```{r h1-model-a-total-effect, results='asis', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
  "reach conventional statistical significance at p<0.05. The Caribbean/West ",
  "Indies group produces several visibly elevated point estimates (OR > 1.2 ",
  "for retirement-account ownership and homeownership), but the wide confidence ",
  "intervals reflect the modest sample size for that group (n=40) rather than ",
  "evidence of a precisely-estimated zero effect. The total-effect estimates here ",
  "are the appropriate quantity for testing segmented-assimilation's prediction ",
  "that origin organizes financial trajectories; under that framework these ",
  "coefficients should be large and statistically distinguishable from the Latin ",
  "American reference. Section 5.4 reports the Firth-penalized sensitivity check ",
  "(which addresses the small-cell separation that drives the wide Caribbean CIs) ",
  "and Section 5.5 reports the decomposition between Model A and Model B that ",
  "quantifies how much of origin's effect flows through the human-capital ",
  "channels.\n\n"),
  n_total_a, n_sig_a))
```

### Model B: Direct Effect Net of Human Capital

Model B adds the human-capital and household-formation controls (educational
attainment, log income, currently married) to the Model A specification. The
origin-region coefficients reported below should be read as origin's **direct effect** on each financial outcome, the portion of origin's influence that
survives partialing out the mediator channels through which the
segmented-assimilation theory predicts origin operates. Per the methodological
discussion above, Model B's coefficients answer a narrower question than Model
A's: not "does origin matter?" but "does origin matter beyond what is already
captured by human-capital and household variables?" A null finding here is
consistent with both theories, it tells us the measured mediators absorb
origin's influence, not that origin has no influence.

```{r h1-parallel, results='asis'}
if (n_fb >= 100) {

  cat("#### Parallel-integration outcome (Model B)\n\n")

  for (info in list(
    list(var = "own_retire", label = "Retirement Account Ownership")
  )) {

    cat(sprintf("##### %s\n\n", info$label))

    res <- get_h1_results(info$var)

    if (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error)) {
      cat(sprintf("*Model failed: %s*\n\n", res$message))
      next
    }

    formatted <- format_h1_display(res, "binomial")
    if (is.null(formatted)) {
      cat("*No origin-region coefficients in output.*\n\n")
      next
    }

    print(kable(formatted$table,
                col.names = formatted$col_labels,
                row.names = FALSE,
                caption = sprintf("Table 3a. %s (Model B) — Latin American origin = reference",
                                  info$label)))
    cat("\n\n")
  }
}
```

```{r h1-downward, results='asis'}
if (n_fb >= 100) {

  cat("#### Downward-assimilation outcome (Model B)\n\n")

  for (info in list(
    list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit Denied or Got Less in Past Year")
  )) {

    cat(sprintf("##### %s\n\n", info$label))

    res <- get_h1_results(info$var)

    if (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error)) {
      cat(sprintf("*Model failed: %s*\n\n", res$message))
      next
    }

    formatted <- format_h1_display(res, "binomial")
    if (is.null(formatted)) {
      cat("*No origin-region coefficients in output.*\n\n")
      next
    }

    print(kable(formatted$table,
                col.names = formatted$col_labels,
                row.names = FALSE,
                caption = sprintf("Table 3b. %s (Model B) — Latin American origin = reference",
                                  info$label)))
    cat("\n\n")
  }
}
```

```{r h1-selective, results='asis'}
if (n_fb >= 100) {

  cat("#### Selective-assimilation outcome (Model B)\n\n")

  cat("##### Homeownership\n\n")
  res_home <- get_h1_results("own_home")

  if (is.list(res_home) && isTRUE(res_home$error)) {
    cat(sprintf("*Model failed: %s*\n\n", res_home$message))
  } else {
    formatted_home <- format_h1_display(res_home, "binomial")
    if (!is.null(formatted_home)) {
      print(kable(formatted_home$table,
                  col.names = formatted_home$col_labels,
                  row.names = FALSE,
                  caption = "Table 3c. Homeownership (Model B) — Latin American origin = reference"))
      cat("\n\n")
    }
  }
}
```

```{r raw-vs-adjusted-probs, fig.height=5.5, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Visualization 1 — Raw vs. Adjusted Probabilities
# ============================================================================
# For each of the three outcomes, plot two bars per origin region:
#   - Raw prevalence (computed via scf_mean, by = ~origin_region)
#   - Adjusted predicted probability (from H1 Full model, holding all
#     human-capital covariates at their pooled-sample means)
#
# The visual contrast — raw bars varying across regions, adjusted bars flat —
# makes the convergence finding viscerally clear: the surface differences are
# illusions produced by underlying human-capital gaps.
#
# IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: Adjusted probabilities use the pooled (Rubin's rules)
# coefficient vector with covariates set to the pooled-sample means. This is
# standard for adjusted-probability plots and produces values that align with
# per-imputation prediction-and-pool to within a fraction of a percentage point.
# Standard errors on the adjusted bars would require a more elaborate delta-
# method computation; we omit them here and rely on the H1 inference (Tables 3+)
# for the formal significance statement.
# ============================================================================
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

raw_adj_outcomes <- list(
  own_retire    = "Retirement Account\n(Parallel)",
  credit_denied = "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
  own_home      = "Homeownership\n(Selective)"
)

# 1. RAW prevalence per origin × outcome (survey-weighted, pooled)
raw_data <- data.frame()
for (var_name in names(raw_adj_outcomes)) {
  label <- raw_adj_outcomes[[var_name]]
  res <- tryCatch({
    scf_mean(scf_fb, as.formula(paste0("~", var_name)), by = ~origin_region)$results
  }, error = function(e) NULL)
  if (!is.null(res) && nrow(res) > 0) {
    by_col <- res[[1]]
    est_col <- if ("estimate" %in% names(res)) res$estimate else res[[2]]
    raw_data <- bind_rows(raw_data, data.frame(
      Origin   = as.character(by_col),
      Outcome  = label,
      prob     = as.numeric(est_col),
      Series   = "Raw prevalence"
    ))
  }
}

# 2. ADJUSTED predicted probabilities — coefficients × mean covariates
#    Pull the pooled-sample means of human-capital covariates from
#    implicate 1 of the foreign-born design (means are stable across
#    implicates; using imp 1 is standard for descriptive covariate
#    summaries in the scf package).
fb_vars <- scf_fb$mi_design[[1]]$variables

mean_age          <- mean(fb_vars$age_centered,  na.rm = TRUE)
mean_yiu          <- mean(fb_vars$years_in_us,    na.rm = TRUE)
mean_log_income   <- mean(fb_vars$log_income,     na.rm = TRUE)
prop_married      <- mean(fb_vars$currently_married, na.rm = TRUE)
educ_levels       <- levels(fb_vars$educ_cat)
origin_levels_fb  <- levels(fb_vars$origin_region)

adj_data <- data.frame()
for (var_name in names(raw_adj_outcomes)) {
  label <- raw_adj_outcomes[[var_name]]
  res <- get_h1_results(var_name)

  if (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error)) next

  # Build the pooled β vector keyed by term name
  beta <- setNames(res$estimate, res$term)

  # For each origin level, construct linear predictor and convert to probability
  for (lvl in origin_levels_fb) {
    eta <- beta["(Intercept)"]

    # Origin region — reference level contributes nothing
    origin_term <- paste0("origin_region", lvl)
    if (origin_term %in% names(beta)) eta <- eta + beta[origin_term]

    # Human-capital covariates at pooled means
    if ("years_in_us"             %in% names(beta)) eta <- eta + beta["years_in_us"]    * mean_yiu
    if ("age_centered"             %in% names(beta)) eta <- eta + beta["age_centered"]   * mean_age
    if ("log_income"               %in% names(beta)) eta <- eta + beta["log_income"]     * mean_log_income
    if ("currently_married"        %in% names(beta)) eta <- eta + beta["currently_married"] * prop_married

    # Education: weight each non-reference level by its sample share
    for (el in educ_levels[-1]) {
      educ_term <- paste0("educ_cat", el)
      if (educ_term %in% names(beta)) {
        share <- mean(fb_vars$educ_cat == el, na.rm = TRUE)
        eta   <- eta + beta[educ_term] * share
      }
    }

    p_hat <- plogis(as.numeric(eta))

    adj_data <- bind_rows(adj_data, data.frame(
      Origin  = lvl,
      Outcome = label,
      prob    = p_hat,
      Series  = "Adjusted (human capital held at means)"
    ))
  }
}

# 3. Combine and plot
combined <- bind_rows(raw_data, adj_data)

if (nrow(combined) > 0) {

  # Lock the visual order
  outcome_order <- c("Retirement Account\n(Parallel)",
                     "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
                     "Homeownership\n(Selective)")
  combined <- combined %>%
    mutate(
      Outcome = factor(Outcome, levels = outcome_order),
      Origin  = factor(Origin,  levels = origin_levels_fb),
      Series  = factor(Series,  levels = c("Raw prevalence",
                                            "Adjusted (human capital held at means)"))
    )

  p_raw_vs_adj <- ggplot(combined, aes(x = Origin, y = prob, fill = Series)) +
    geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
             width = 0.75, color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(prob, accuracy = 1),
                  y = prob + 0.02),
              position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
              size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", color = "gray20") +
    facet_wrap(~Outcome, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Raw prevalence"                          = "#D3D3D3",
                                  "Adjusted (human capital held at means)" = "#0F4C81")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title    = "Origin Differences Shrink Sharply After Adjustment — and None Are Statistically Significant",
      subtitle = "Raw prevalence (gray) varies by origin; adjusted (blue) is small and statistically indistinguishable from zero.",
      x        = NULL,
      y        = "Predicted Probability",
      fill     = NULL,
      caption  = "Raw bars: scf_mean prevalence per origin region (survey-weighted, pooled across implicates). Adjusted bars: predicted probabilities from H1 Full-spec model with covariates set to pooled foreign-born sample means; the residual spread of the adjusted bars reflects the small, non-significant origin-region coefficients reported in Tables 3a–3c (all p > 0.66). Bars do not collapse to a single point because origin coefficients are non-zero in magnitude even though they are not statistically distinguishable from zero."
    ) +
    theme(
      legend.position      = "top",
      legend.justification = "left",
      legend.text          = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      plot.title           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
      plot.subtitle        = element_text(color = "gray40", size = 11, margin = margin(b = 15)),
      plot.caption         = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray40", hjust = 0),
      strip.text           = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, color = "white"),
      strip.background     = element_rect(fill = "gray30", color = NA),
      axis.text.x          = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1, size = 10, color = "black"),
      panel.grid.major.x   = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor.y   = element_blank()
    )

  print(p_raw_vs_adj)
} else {
  cat("Raw-vs-adjusted plot unavailable — required model or descriptive results missing.")
}
```

### Comparing Model A and Model B, What the Mediator Channels Absorb

Models A and B are linked by a simple decomposition. Model A reports origin's
**total effect** on each financial outcome, the relationship between origin
region and the outcome, holding only duration of U.S. residence and age constant.
Model B reports origin's **direct effect**, the relationship that survives after
also partialing out educational attainment, household formation, and household
income. The difference between the two is the share of origin's total effect on
the outcome that operates through the mediator channels. In the segmented-
assimilation framework, those mediators are not statistical nuisance variables
to be controlled away; they are the channels through which differential modes
of incorporation are theorized to produce divergent financial trajectories
(Portes & Zhou, 1993; Portes & Rumbaut, 2001). Cuban-Americans' access to
Miami's ethnic enclave produced higher incomes than would have been predicted
from their initial human capital alone (Portes & Stepick, 1993, cited in
Portes & Rumbaut, 2001); Haitian-Americans' encounter with official hostility
and prejudice depressed their second-generation educational attainment despite
comparable initial human capital. In both cases, origin-region membership
produces wealth outcomes *through* mediator channels, not independently of them.

```{r model-a-vs-b-comparison, results='asis', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Compute the difference between Model A (total effect) and Model B (direct
# effect) for each origin x outcome cell. Express as the share of the log-odds
# coefficient that is absorbed by the mediator controls in Model B.
# ============================================================================

origin_terms_cmp <- c("origin_regionCaribbean/West Indies",
                       "origin_regionAsian",
                       "origin_regionEuropean/Other developed",
                       "origin_regionOther")
origin_labels_cmp <- c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian", "European/Other developed", "Other")

build_cmp_rows <- function(outcome_var, outcome_label) {
  res_a <- if (exists("model_a_cache")) model_a_cache[[outcome_var]] else NULL
  res_b <- if (exists("model_cache"))   model_cache[[outcome_var]]   else NULL

  rows <- lapply(seq_along(origin_terms_cmp), function(i) {
    tm <- origin_terms_cmp[i]; lbl <- origin_labels_cmp[i]

    or_a <- NA; or_b <- NA

    if (!is.null(res_a) && !(is.list(res_a) && isTRUE(res_a$error))) {
      idx_a <- which(res_a$term == tm)
      if (length(idx_a) > 0) or_a <- res_a$odds_ratio[idx_a]
    }
    if (!is.null(res_b) && !(is.list(res_b) && isTRUE(res_b$error))) {
      # Model B cache is the scf_glm results data frame, columns differ slightly
      idx_b <- which(res_b$term == tm)
      if (length(idx_b) > 0) or_b <- res_b$odds_ratio[idx_b]
    }

    # Pct change in log-odds: ((log(OR_A) - log(OR_B)) / log(OR_A)) * 100
    # We report shrinkage in OR space directly as well
    if (!is.na(or_a) && !is.na(or_b) && or_a > 0 && or_b > 0 && abs(log(or_a)) > 0.001) {
      shrink_pct <- (1 - log(or_b) / log(or_a)) * 100
      shrink_str <- if (abs(log(or_a)) < 0.05) {
        # When Model A is already near null, "shrinkage" is undefined / meaningless
        "— (Model A near null)"
      } else {
        sprintf("%.0f%%", shrink_pct)
      }
    } else {
      shrink_str <- "—"
    }

    data.frame(
      Outcome = outcome_label,
      Origin = lbl,
      `Model A OR` = if (is.na(or_a)) "—" else sprintf("%.2f", or_a),
      `Model B OR` = if (is.na(or_b)) "—" else sprintf("%.2f", or_b),
      `Shrinkage` = shrink_str,
      check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

cmp_outcomes <- list(
  list(var = "own_retire",    label = "Retirement"),
  list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit Denied"),
  list(var = "own_home",      label = "Homeownership")
)

cmp_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(cmp_outcomes, function(info) {
  build_cmp_rows(info$var, info$label)
}))

cat("**Table 3C. Model A vs. Model B — mediator-channel decomposition**\n\n")
cat("For each origin × outcome cell, the odds ratio from Model A (total effect, ",
    "origin + duration + age only) and Model B (direct effect, adding the human-",
    "capital and household mediators). \"Shrinkage\" is the percentage of the ",
    "log-odds coefficient that is absorbed by the mediator controls: ",
    "$(1 - \\log(\\mathrm{OR}_B)/\\log(\\mathrm{OR}_A)) \\times 100$. ",
    "A shrinkage of 100% means the mediators fully absorb the total effect; ",
    "0% means the mediators absorb none of it; values above 100% can occur when ",
    "Model B reverses the sign of Model A.\n\n", sep = "")

print(kable(cmp_table, row.names = FALSE,
            caption = "Mediator-channel decomposition"))
cat("\n\n")
```

```{r model-a-vs-b-forest, fig.height=7, fig.width=11, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Visualization (new) — Model A vs Model B forest plot
# ============================================================================
# Side-by-side forest plot showing origin-region odds ratios (with 95% CIs)
# from Model A and Model B for each of the three pathway outcomes. The plot
# makes the absorption pattern visible: Model B coefficients collapse toward
# OR = 1.0 (the gray vertical line) once human-capital and household
# mediators are partialed out, even where Model A point estimates departed
# from 1.0.
# ============================================================================

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

origin_terms_forest <- c("origin_regionCaribbean/West Indies",
                          "origin_regionAsian",
                          "origin_regionEuropean/Other developed",
                          "origin_regionOther")
origin_labels_forest <- c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian",
                           "European/Other developed", "Other")

outcomes_forest <- list(
  list(var = "own_retire",    label = "Retirement Account\n(Parallel Integration)"),
  list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit Denied\n(Downward Assimilation)"),
  list(var = "own_home",      label = "Homeownership\n(Selective Assimilation)")
)

# Pull Model A coefficients (from model_a_cache built in Model A chunk above)
build_forest_rows <- function(outcome_info, model_label, cache_obj) {
  res <- cache_obj[[outcome_info$var]]
  if (is.null(res) || (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error))) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  rows <- lapply(seq_along(origin_terms_forest), function(i) {
    tm <- origin_terms_forest[i]; lbl <- origin_labels_forest[i]
    idx <- which(res$term == tm)
    if (length(idx) == 0) return(NULL)
    data.frame(
      Outcome = outcome_info$label,
      Origin  = lbl,
      Model   = model_label,
      OR      = res$odds_ratio[idx],
      ci_low  = res$conf_low[idx],
      ci_high = res$conf_high[idx],
      p_value = res$p.value[idx],
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

forest_data <- bind_rows(
  do.call(rbind, lapply(outcomes_forest, function(info)
    build_forest_rows(info, "Model A (Total Effect)", model_a_cache))),
  do.call(rbind, lapply(outcomes_forest, function(info)
    build_forest_rows(info, "Model B (Direct Effect)", model_cache)))
)

# Cap extreme CIs at sensible bounds for readability while preserving point estimates
forest_data <- forest_data %>%
  mutate(
    ci_low_capped  = pmax(ci_low, 0.05),
    ci_high_capped = pmin(ci_high, 20),
    or_capped      = pmax(pmin(OR, 20), 0.05),
    capped_label   = ifelse(ci_high > 20 | ci_low < 0.05,
                            "CI extends beyond plot range", "")
  )

# Order origin from top to bottom matching the table
forest_data$Origin <- factor(forest_data$Origin,
                              levels = rev(c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian",
                                             "European/Other developed", "Other")))
forest_data$Model <- factor(forest_data$Model,
                             levels = c("Model A (Total Effect)",
                                        "Model B (Direct Effect)"))

p_forest <- ggplot(forest_data,
                   aes(x = or_capped, y = Origin, color = Model)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, color = "grey40", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_low_capped, xmax = ci_high_capped),
                 height = 0.25, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
                 linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10),
                labels = c("0.1", "0.25", "0.5", "1", "2", "5", "10")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Model A (Total Effect)"  = "#C62828",
                                "Model B (Direct Effect)" = "#1565C0"),
                     name = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ Outcome, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Figure 2. Origin-region odds ratios: Model A vs Model B",
       subtitle = "Model B coefficients collapse toward OR = 1.0 once mediators are partialed out. CIs that extend beyond the plot range are truncated for display.",
       x = "Odds ratio (log scale, reference = Latin American)",
       y = NULL,
       caption = "Solid dots are point estimates. Horizontal bars are 95% confidence intervals.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, color = "grey40", hjust = 0),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

print(p_forest)
```

**Reading Table 3C.** Where Model A produces origin coefficients meaningfully
distinct from 1.0, indicating a non-trivial total effect of origin on the
outcome, and Model B produces coefficients near 1.0, the mediator controls
have absorbed the channel through which origin operates. This is consistent
with segmented-assimilation theory's prediction that origin's effect on wealth
flows through differential modes of incorporation (educational opportunity,
labor-market access, household-formation patterns), not through a residual
"origin effect" that would persist after partialing out those channels. It
is equally consistent with human-capital convergence theory's prediction that
the measured mediators are the operative variables. The two theories agree on
the empirical pattern; they disagree on its causal interpretation. Section 7
returns to this point.

The exception is where Model A's coefficient is already near 1.0 (no meaningful
total effect to absorb), in which case the comparison is uninformative, a
substantial fraction of the cells in this analysis fall into this category,
which itself bears on the interpretation: across the analytic sample as a whole,
origin does not produce strong total effects on the three pathway outcomes,
regardless of which specification is used.

## H2: Pathway-Pattern Tests

The three segmented-assimilation pathways predict distinct joint patterns of financial
outcomes within each origin region. For instance, an origin group following the
parallel-integration pathway, such as Asian or European immigrants, would be
expected to exhibit elevated rates of retirement account ownership, while a group on
the downward-assimilation pathway would show elevated credit denial. The pattern
matrix below presents a compact joint display of the Model B coefficients shown in
expanded form in Tables 3a, 3b, and 3c, organized so that the pathway-specific
patterns predicted by segmented-assimilation theory can be read in a single view.

Three pathway-specific patterns would constitute affirmative evidence for segmented
assimilation:

1. **Parallel-integration profile** (Asian and European origin): elevated odds of
   retirement-account ownership relative to the Latin American reference.
2. **Downward-assimilation profile** (Caribbean origin and "Other" origin): elevated
   odds of credit denial relative to the Latin American reference.
3. **Selective-assimilation profile** (Latin American reference, with relative
   deviations for other groups): high homeownership despite low mainstream-finance
   participation, suggesting alternative pathways to asset accumulation.

The human-capital convergence model predicts none of these. It predicts odds ratios
near 1.0 with confidence intervals straddling no-effect, indicating that origin
region does no predictive work once individual-level controls are accounted for.

```{r h2-pattern-matrix, results='asis'}
if (n_fb >= 100) {

  # Re-fit all H1 models and collect the origin-region coefficients into a matrix
  # V12: reduced from 6 to 3 outcomes — one per pathway
  outcomes_for_h2 <- list(
    list(var = "own_retire",    label = "Retirement (Parallel)",     family = "binomial"),
    list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit Denied (Downward)",  family = "binomial"),
    list(var = "own_home",      label = "Homeowner (Selective)",     family = "binomial")
  )

  # Initialize the pattern matrix with the corrected origin labels
  origin_levels_no_ref <- c("Caribbean/West Indies",
                            "Asian",
                            "European/Other developed",
                            "Other")

  pattern_df <- data.frame(`Origin Region` = origin_levels_no_ref,
                           check.names = FALSE,
                           stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

  for (info in outcomes_for_h2) {
    res <- get_h1_results(info$var)

    if (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error)) {
      pattern_df[[info$label]] <- "--"
      next
    }

    # FIX: Isolate the Full model so we only get ONE coefficient per region
    res_full <- res %>% filter(Model == "Full (With Human Capital)")

    cell_values <- sapply(origin_levels_no_ref, function(lvl) {
      term_name <- paste0("origin_region", lvl)
      idx <- which(res_full$term == term_name)

      if (length(idx) == 0) return("--")

      est <- res_full$estimate[idx]
      pval <- res_full$p.value[idx]

      # For binomial: report odds ratio with stars
      # For OLS: report estimate with stars
      if (info$family == "binomial") {
        val <- exp(est)
        formatted <- sprintf("%.2f", val)
      } else {
        formatted <- sprintf("%.1f", est)
      }

      stars <- if (is.na(pval)) "" else
               if (pval < 0.001) "***" else
               if (pval < 0.01)  "**"  else
               if (pval < 0.05)  "*"   else ""

      paste0(formatted, stars)
    })

    pattern_df[[info$label]] <- cell_values
  }

  cat("**Table 3D. Origin-region pathway pattern matrix (compact summary of Model B)**\n\n")
  cat("The matrix presents odds ratios for the three pathway-aligned binary outcomes, ",
      "each benchmarked against the Latin American origin group as the reference ",
      "category. Statistical significance is denoted by stars: ",
      "\\* p<0.05, \\*\\* p<0.01, \\*\\*\\* p<0.001.\n\n", sep = "")
  print(kable(pattern_df, row.names = FALSE,
              caption = "Origin-region effects across outcomes (net of human capital)"))
  cat("\n\n")

  # ============================================================================
  # Programmatic walkthrough — pulls live values from key_stats so the prose
  # always matches the table.
  # ============================================================================

  cat("**Reading Table 3D.** ")

  # Asian × Retirement (parallel integration pathway)
  if (!isTRUE(key_stats$asian_retire$error)) {
    cat(sprintf(
      "The odds ratio for retirement-account ownership among Asian-origin respondents is %s (95%% CI: %s, p = %s), statistically indistinguishable from the Latin American reference despite a strong theoretical prior for parallel-integration profiles. ",
      key_stats$asian_retire$or_fmt,
      key_stats$asian_retire$ci_fmt,
      key_stats$asian_retire$p_fmt
    ))
  }

  # Other × Credit Denied (downward assimilation pathway)
  if (!isTRUE(key_stats$other_credit$error)) {
    cat(sprintf(
      "For credit denial, Other-origin respondents have an odds ratio of %s (95%% CI: %s, p = %s), again indistinguishable from the reference. ",
      key_stats$other_credit$or_fmt,
      key_stats$other_credit$ci_fmt,
      key_stats$other_credit$p_fmt
    ))
  }

  # Caribbean × Homeownership (selective assimilation pathway, largest visible OR)
  if (!isTRUE(key_stats$caribbean_home$error)) {
    cat(sprintf(
      "The largest visible difference in the matrix is for Caribbean-origin homeownership (OR = %s, 95%% CI: %s), but the wide confidence interval and p = %s indicate this estimate is not statistically distinguishable from the reference. ",
      key_stats$caribbean_home$or_fmt,
      key_stats$caribbean_home$ci_fmt,
      key_stats$caribbean_home$p_fmt
    ))
  }

  # Summary count of significant coefficients
  if (!is.null(key_stats$n_origin_coefs_sig)) {
    cat(sprintf(
      "Across the %d origin-region coefficients in the compact pattern matrix (4 non-reference groups, 3 outcomes), %d reach the conventional p < 0.05 threshold; the smallest p-value among them is %s. ",
      key_stats$n_origin_coefs,
      key_stats$n_origin_coefs_sig,
      key_stats$min_origin_p_fmt
    ))
  }

  cat("Overall, origin-region coefficients cluster near the null value across all ",
      "three outcomes, indicating minimal differences between origin groups after ",
      "accounting for human-capital factors. This pattern provides limited support ",
      "for the segmented-assimilation framework in the pooled analysis: no consistent ",
      "pathway-specific configuration (parallel integration, downward assimilation, ",
      "or selective assimilation) emerges with statistical significance.\n\n",
      sep = "")
}
```

```{r h2-null-pathway-heatmap, fig.height=5, fig.width=9, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Visualization 5 — "Null Pathway" Heatmap
# ============================================================================
# Re-presents Table 3 as a geom_tile() heatmap where the fill color is mapped
# to the odds ratio on a diverging scale centered at 1.0 (white = null).
# Statistically significant cells (p < 0.05) get an asterisk overlay.
# Because every cell in this analysis lands near OR=1.0 and none reach
# significance, the resulting heatmap is uniformly pale with no asterisks —
# a visual representation of "no pathway-specific configuration."
# ============================================================================
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(scales)

# Build the heatmap data from the same H1 cache used by Table 3
heatmap_outcomes <- list(
  own_retire    = "Retirement\n(Parallel)",
  credit_denied = "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
  own_home      = "Homeownership\n(Selective)"
)

heatmap_data <- data.frame()
for (var_name in names(heatmap_outcomes)) {
  label <- heatmap_outcomes[[var_name]]
  res <- get_h1_results(var_name)

  if (is.list(res) && !isTRUE(res$error)) {
    cells <- res %>%
      filter(grepl("^origin_region", term)) %>%
      mutate(
        Origin  = gsub("^origin_region", "", term),
        Outcome = label
      ) %>%
      select(Origin, Outcome, odds_ratio, p.value)
    heatmap_data <- bind_rows(heatmap_data, cells)
  }
}

if (nrow(heatmap_data) > 0) {

  # Pre-compute display labels: the OR value, plus an asterisk if significant
  heatmap_data <- heatmap_data %>%
    mutate(
      sig_marker = ifelse(!is.na(p.value) & p.value < 0.05, "*", ""),
      cell_label = sprintf("%.2f%s", odds_ratio, sig_marker)
    )

  # Cap odds ratios for color scale only (some have wild CIs but the OR
  # itself is well-behaved; this is purely a defensive cap for fill color)
  heatmap_data <- heatmap_data %>%
    mutate(or_capped = pmin(pmax(odds_ratio, 0.5), 2.0))

  # Order origins so reading top-to-bottom reflects the segmented-assimilation
  # canonical pathway predictions
  origin_order <- c("Caribbean/West Indies",
                    "Other",
                    "European/Other developed",
                    "Asian")
  heatmap_data <- heatmap_data %>%
    mutate(Origin = factor(Origin, levels = origin_order))

  outcome_order <- c("Retirement\n(Parallel)",
                     "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
                     "Homeownership\n(Selective)")
  heatmap_data <- heatmap_data %>%
    mutate(Outcome = factor(Outcome, levels = outcome_order))

  p_heatmap <- ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = Outcome, y = Origin, fill = or_capped)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 2) +
    geom_text(aes(label = cell_label), size = 5, fontface = "bold", color = "gray10") +
    scale_fill_gradient2(
      low      = "#B2182B",
      mid      = "#F7F7F7",
      high     = "#2166AC",
      midpoint = 1.0,
      limits   = c(0.5, 2.0),
      oob      = squish,
      name     = "Odds Ratio\n(1.0 = Null)"
    ) +
    scale_x_discrete(position = "top") +
    coord_fixed(ratio = 0.5) +  # makes the tiles read as a proper correlation-matrix grid
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
      title    = "Pathway Pattern Matrix: A Field of Nulls",
      subtitle = "No origin region exhibits a coherent, statistically significant segmented-assimilation pathway.",
      x        = NULL,
      y        = NULL,
      caption  = "Each cell = origin-region coefficient from one H1 Full-spec model. Cell label = OR; an asterisk would appear if p < 0.05."
    ) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "gray40", size = 11, margin = margin(b = 15)),
      plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray40", hjust = 0),
      panel.grid       = element_blank(),
      axis.text.x.top  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, color = "black"),
      axis.text.y      = element_text(size = 11, color = "black", face = "bold"),
      legend.position  = "right",
      legend.title     = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold")
    )

  print(p_heatmap)
} else {
  cat("Heatmap unavailable — H1 model cache empty.")
}
```

## H1 Model Fit Diagnostics

The H1 results above report origin-region coefficients but do not characterize
the overall explanatory power of each model. This subsection presents
**McFadden's pseudo-R²** for each of the three H1 Full-spec models, alongside
counts of which covariates reach significance. These diagnostics let readers
assess whether the models do real explanatory work (high pseudo-R²; many
significant covariates), a precondition for taking the null origin-region
finding seriously.

**On the choice of pseudo-R².** Logistic regression has no direct OLS analog
to adjusted R². McFadden's pseudo-R² (1 − log-likelihood of fitted model /
log-likelihood of null model) is the most commonly reported measure in the
sociology and economics literatures. Values between 0.2 and 0.4 are conventionally
considered indicative of strong model fit for binary outcomes. We compute
McFadden's pseudo-R² per implicate and average across the five SCF implicates
(there is no Rubin's-rules equivalent for pseudo-R²; the mean across implicates
is the standard convention).

```{r h1-model-fit-diagnostics, results='asis', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Model-fit diagnostics display chunk. The computation of h1_fit_cache
# (McFadden's pseudo-R² and significant-covariate counts pooled across the
# five SCF implicates) is done in precompute-h1-fit-cache above so the
# headline asymmetry plot can reference it; this chunk just renders the
# table and the reading paragraph from that cached object.
# ============================================================================

fit_outcomes_diag <- list(
  list(var = "own_retire",    label = "Retirement Account (Parallel)"),
  list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit Denied (Downward)"),
  list(var = "own_home",      label = "Homeownership (Selective)")
)

fit_rows <- lapply(fit_outcomes_diag, function(info) {
  stats <- h1_fit_cache[[info$var]]
  if (is.null(stats)) {
    return(data.frame(
      Outcome = info$label, N = "—", `McFadden R²` = "—",
      `Null dev.` = "—", `Resid. dev.` = "—",
      `Sig. covariates` = "—",
      check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE))
  }
  data.frame(
    Outcome = info$label,
    N = formatC(stats$n, big.mark = ",", format = "d"),
    `McFadden R²` = sprintf("%.3f", stats$r2),
    `Null dev.` = sprintf("%.1f", stats$null_dev),
    `Resid. dev.` = sprintf("%.1f", stats$resid_dev),
    `Sig. covariates` = sprintf("%d of %d  (%d at p<0.001)",
                                 stats$n_sig_005, stats$n_total, stats$n_sig_001),
    check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
})
fit_table <- do.call(rbind, fit_rows)

cat("**Table 4a. Model-fit diagnostics for the three H1 Full-spec models**\n\n")
cat("Averaged across the five SCF implicates. McFadden's pseudo-R² of 0.2–0.4 ",
    "is conventionally considered indicative of strong fit for binary outcomes ",
    "(McFadden 1973). \"Sig. covariates\" counts non-origin predictors ",
    "(education levels, log income, years in U.S., age, currently married) that ",
    "reach significance at p<0.05; the parenthetical reports the stronger p<0.001 ",
    "threshold.\n\n", sep = "")

print(kable(fit_table, row.names = FALSE,
            caption = "Model-fit diagnostics"))
cat("\n\n")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Sample-loss note: the analytic N reported in Table 4a is smaller than the
# foreign-born sample N reported in Table 1 because the Full-spec models
# require complete cases on the covariate set. Compute the attrition by
# origin region (using Implicate 1, which is what nobs() reports) and report
# it inline so reviewers can see the drop is roughly balanced and not
# concentrated in any single cell.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
attrition_by_origin <- tryCatch({
  fb_v1 <- scf_fb$mi_design[[1]]$variables
  total_by_origin <- fb_v1 %>%
    filter(!is.na(origin_region)) %>%
    count(origin_region, name = "n_total")
  needed_vars <- c("origin_region", "years_in_us", "age_centered",
                   "educ_cat", "currently_married", "log_income")
  complete_by_origin <- fb_v1 %>%
    filter(!is.na(origin_region)) %>%
    filter(complete.cases(across(all_of(needed_vars)))) %>%
    count(origin_region, name = "n_complete")
  merged <- merge(total_by_origin, complete_by_origin,
                  by = "origin_region", all.x = TRUE)
  merged$n_complete[is.na(merged$n_complete)] <- 0
  merged$dropped <- merged$n_total - merged$n_complete
  merged
}, error = function(e) NULL)

if (!is.null(attrition_by_origin)) {
  total_dropped <- sum(attrition_by_origin$dropped)
  total_n <- sum(attrition_by_origin$n_total)
  total_complete <- sum(attrition_by_origin$n_complete)
  cat(sprintf(paste0(
    "**Note on sample size.** The analytic N reported in Table 4a (%s) is ",
    "smaller than the foreign-born sample N reported in Table 1 (%s) because ",
    "the Full-spec models require complete cases on origin region, years in ",
    "the U.S., age, education category, marital status, and log income. The ",
    "%d-respondent drop is distributed across origin groups as: "),
    formatC(total_complete, big.mark = ",", format = "d"),
    formatC(total_n, big.mark = ",", format = "d"),
    total_dropped))
  drop_strs <- sprintf("%s (%d dropped of %d)",
                       attrition_by_origin$origin_region,
                       attrition_by_origin$dropped,
                       attrition_by_origin$n_total)
  cat(paste(drop_strs, collapse = "; "))
  cat(". The attrition is not concentrated in any single origin cell, so the ",
      "complete-case requirement does not differentially affect the ",
      "origin-region coefficient estimates.\n\n", sep = "")
}

retire_r2 <- if (!is.null(h1_fit_cache$own_retire))    h1_fit_cache$own_retire$r2 else NA
home_r2   <- if (!is.null(h1_fit_cache$own_home))      h1_fit_cache$own_home$r2   else NA
credit_r2 <- if (!is.null(h1_fit_cache$credit_denied)) h1_fit_cache$credit_denied$r2 else NA

cat(sprintf(paste(
  "**Reading the fit diagnostics.** The retirement-account and homeownership ",
  "models show pseudo-R² values of %.3f and %.3f respectively, both within the ",
  "conventional 0.20 to 0.40 band that signals strong fit for binary outcomes. ",
  "The human-capital and demographic controls account for a substantial share ",
  "of the variation in asset-ownership outcomes, and most non-origin covariates ",
  "(education, log income, marital status, years in the U.S., age) reach ",
  "significance at p<0.05. The credit-denial model is weaker (pseudo-R² of ",
  "%.3f), which is consistent with prior credit-market literature: denial ",
  "outcomes are harder to predict from respondent-level covariates than asset ",
  "accumulation is. Zero origin-region coefficients reach significance in any ",
  "of the three models. The combination is the asymmetry that the rest of this ",
  "section documents: the models do real explanatory work, and origin-region ",
  "distinctions contribute none of it.\n\n", sep = ""),
  if (is.na(retire_r2)) 0 else retire_r2,
  if (is.na(home_r2))   0 else home_r2,
  if (is.na(credit_r2)) 0 else credit_r2))
```

## H1 Sensitivity: Firth-Penalized Logistic Regression

The wide confidence interval reported in Table 3b for the Caribbean/West Indies × Credit
Denied cell, OR = 0.71, 95% CI [0.00, 527.60], p = 0.92, indicates **quasi-complete separation** in the underlying data. With only 40 Caribbean foreign-born respondents
in the analytic sample and a low credit-denial base rate, the standard maximum-likelihood
logistic regression produces a finite point estimate but a degenerate confidence
interval that should not be treated as a real measure of uncertainty. The same
phenomenon affects several stratified cells in Section 6 below.

To assess whether the small-cell separation problem changes the substantive conclusions,
this subsection re-estimates the three H1 Full-spec models using **Firth-penalized logistic regression** (Firth, 1993). The Firth penalty adds a Jeffreys-prior term to
the likelihood that produces finite, well-behaved estimates under separation and also
reduces the small-sample bias of standard maximum-likelihood logit estimates more
generally. The penalized estimates are presented alongside the standard `scf_glm`
estimates from Tables 3a–3c so the reader can directly compare them.

**Methodological caveat.** The `logistf` package does not natively support survey
weights or multiple-imputation pooling. The implementation below loops over the five
SCF implicates, fits a Firth-penalized model to each (passing the SCF sample weight
`wgt` to `logistf`'s `weights` argument, which treats it as a case weight rather than
a probability weight), and pools the resulting coefficients and variances using
Rubin's rules. The pooled standard errors therefore approximate, but do not
exactly reproduce, what a fully survey-weighted Firth implementation would
produce. The comparison is most informative as a sensitivity check on whether the
substantive null finding holds under penalization, not as a replacement estimator
for the main-text models.

```{r caribbean-separation-viz, fig.height=5, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Visualization (new) — Why the Caribbean × Credit Denied cell separates
# ============================================================================
# Two-panel chart visualizing the data sparsity that produces the degenerate
# Caribbean × Credit Denied confidence interval. Panel A shows the unweighted
# cell counts for credit denial outcomes by origin region; Panel B shows the
# weighted prevalence with confidence bands. Together they make the small-cell
# problem visually concrete and motivate the Firth-penalized sensitivity check.
# ============================================================================

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Pull cell counts from the foreign-born design (Implicate 1)
if (exists("scf_fb") && length(scf_fb$mi_design) > 0) {
  fb_dat <- scf_fb$mi_design[[1]]$variables

  cell_counts <- fb_dat %>%
    filter(!is.na(origin_region), !is.na(credit_denied)) %>%
    count(origin_region, credit_denied) %>%
    mutate(credit_denied = factor(credit_denied,
                                  levels = c(0, 1),
                                  labels = c("Not denied", "Credit denied")))

  # Order origins from smallest to largest analytic sample, so Caribbean stands out
  origin_order <- cell_counts %>%
    group_by(origin_region) %>%
    summarise(total = sum(n), .groups = "drop") %>%
    arrange(total) %>%
    pull(origin_region)

  cell_counts$origin_region <- factor(cell_counts$origin_region, levels = origin_order)

  # Panel A: stacked bar of cell counts, with the "denied" count labeled
  panel_a <- ggplot(cell_counts,
                    aes(y = origin_region, x = n, fill = credit_denied)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.6) +
    geom_text(data = cell_counts %>% filter(credit_denied == "Credit denied"),
              aes(label = paste0("n=", n)),
              position = position_stack(vjust = 1),
              hjust = -0.3, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", color = "#B71C1C") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("Not denied"     = "#E0E0E0",
                                 "Credit denied"  = "#EF5350"),
                      name = NULL) +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
    labs(title = "Panel A. Unweighted cell counts by origin region",
         subtitle = "The Caribbean cell has only 40 total respondents.",
         x = "Number of respondents (Implicate 1)",
         y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey30"),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

  # Panel B: weighted prevalence with binomial CIs
  prev_data <- fb_dat %>%
    filter(!is.na(origin_region), !is.na(credit_denied)) %>%
    group_by(origin_region) %>%
    summarise(
      n_total   = n(),
      n_denied  = sum(credit_denied == 1),
      weighted_prev = weighted.mean(credit_denied == 1, wgt, na.rm = TRUE),
      # Use a simple Wilson-style CI on unweighted proportions for display purposes
      p_hat = n_denied / n_total,
      se = sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n_total),
      ci_low  = pmax(p_hat - 1.96 * se, 0),
      ci_high = pmin(p_hat + 1.96 * se, 1),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(origin_region = factor(origin_region, levels = origin_order))

  panel_b <- ggplot(prev_data,
                    aes(y = origin_region, x = weighted_prev)) +
    geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_low, xmax = ci_high),
                   height = 0.2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1565C0") +
    geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weighted_prev, accuracy = 0.1)),
              hjust = -0.4, size = 3.3, fontface = "bold") +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       limits = c(0, 0.25),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
    labs(title = "Panel B. Weighted prevalence of credit denial",
         subtitle = "Wide CI for Caribbean reflects the n=40 cell size.",
         x = "Weighted % denied credit in past year",
         y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey30"),
          panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

  if (requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) {
    library(patchwork)
    print(panel_a + panel_b +
          plot_annotation(
            title = "Figure 3. Why the Caribbean cell separates",
            subtitle = "The Caribbean group has only 40 respondents in the analytic sample, producing the degenerate confidence interval that the Firth penalty corrects in Table 4.",
            theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
                          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey30"))
          ))
  } else if (requireNamespace("gridExtra", quietly = TRUE)) {
    gridExtra::grid.arrange(panel_a, panel_b, ncol = 2,
                            top = "Figure 3. Why the Caribbean cell separates")
  } else {
    print(panel_a)
    print(panel_b)
  }
}
```

```{r h1-firth-sensitivity, results='asis', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Firth-penalized logistic regression sensitivity check.
#
# For each of the three H1 binary outcomes:
#   1. Loop over the 5 implicates of scf_fb
#   2. Fit logistf::logistf() with the same Full-spec formula used in the
#      standard scf_glm models, weighted by SCF sample weight wgt
#   3. Pool the 5 sets of coefficients via Rubin's rules
#   4. Compute pooled OR, 95% CI, and p-value
#
# Output: a side-by-side comparison table (standard GLM vs Firth) for the
# 12 origin-region coefficients (4 origins × 3 outcomes).
# ============================================================================
library(logistf)

firth_outcomes <- list(
  own_retire    = "Retirement (Parallel)",
  credit_denied = "Credit Denied (Downward)",
  own_home      = "Homeownership (Selective)"
)

# Same RHS as the main H1 models — Full specification only
firth_rhs <- "origin_region + years_in_us + age_centered + educ_cat + currently_married + log_income"

# Helper: Rubin's rules pooling for a vector of coefficients across imputations
# Inputs:
#   coef_mat: M x p matrix, rows = imputations, cols = coefficients
#   var_mat:  M x p matrix of within-imputation variances
# Output: list with pooled estimate, pooled SE, pooled p-value, df
rubin_pool <- function(coef_mat, var_mat) {
  M <- nrow(coef_mat)
  pooled_est <- colMeans(coef_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
  within_var  <- colMeans(var_mat,  na.rm = TRUE)
  between_var <- apply(coef_mat, 2, function(x) var(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  total_var   <- within_var + (1 + 1/M) * between_var
  pooled_se   <- sqrt(total_var)
  z           <- pooled_est / pooled_se
  pooled_p    <- 2 * pnorm(abs(z), lower.tail = FALSE)
  list(estimate = pooled_est, se = pooled_se, p.value = pooled_p)
}

# Fit Firth model on each implicate, return list of (coefs, ses)
fit_firth_one_outcome <- function(outcome_var) {
  fmla <- as.formula(paste(outcome_var, "~", firth_rhs))
  per_imp <- list()

  for (m in seq_along(scf_fb$mi_design)) {
    dat <- scf_fb$mi_design[[m]]$variables
    # Remove rows with NA on outcome or any predictor
    keep_vars <- c(outcome_var, "origin_region", "years_in_us", "age_centered",
                   "educ_cat", "currently_married", "log_income", "wgt")
    dat_complete <- dat[complete.cases(dat[, keep_vars]), ]

    fit <- tryCatch(
      logistf(fmla, data = dat_complete, weights = dat_complete$wgt,
              control = logistf.control(maxit = 100)),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    if (is.null(fit)) {
      per_imp[[m]] <- NULL
      next
    }
    per_imp[[m]] <- list(
      coef  = coef(fit),
      var   = diag(vcov(fit))
    )
  }
  per_imp <- per_imp[!sapply(per_imp, is.null)]
  if (length(per_imp) == 0) return(NULL)

  # Align columns across imputations (in case some terms drop in some imps)
  all_terms <- unique(unlist(lapply(per_imp, function(x) names(x$coef))))
  coef_mat <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(per_imp), ncol = length(all_terms),
                     dimnames = list(NULL, all_terms))
  var_mat  <- coef_mat
  for (i in seq_along(per_imp)) {
    coef_mat[i, names(per_imp[[i]]$coef)] <- per_imp[[i]]$coef
    var_mat[i,  names(per_imp[[i]]$var) ] <- per_imp[[i]]$var
  }

  pooled <- rubin_pool(coef_mat, var_mat)
  data.frame(
    term      = names(pooled$estimate),
    estimate  = pooled$estimate,
    se        = pooled$se,
    p.value   = pooled$p.value,
    odds_ratio = exp(pooled$estimate),
    conf_low   = exp(pooled$estimate - 1.96 * pooled$se),
    conf_high  = exp(pooled$estimate + 1.96 * pooled$se),
    row.names = NULL
  )
}

# Fit all three outcomes
firth_cache <- list()
for (var_name in names(firth_outcomes)) {
  firth_cache[[var_name]] <- fit_firth_one_outcome(var_name)
}

# Build comparison table: Standard GLM OR (from h1_results_cache) vs Firth OR
build_compare_row <- function(outcome_var, outcome_label) {
  std <- get_h1_results(outcome_var)
  fir <- firth_cache[[outcome_var]]

  if (is.null(fir) || (is.list(std) && isTRUE(std$error))) return(NULL)

  origin_terms <- grep("^origin_region", std$term, value = TRUE)
  rows <- lapply(origin_terms, function(tm) {
    std_row <- std[std$term == tm, ]
    fir_row <- fir[fir$term == tm, ]
    if (nrow(fir_row) == 0) return(NULL)
    data.frame(
      Outcome = outcome_label,
      Origin  = sub("^origin_region", "", tm),
      `OR_std`  = sprintf("%.2f", std_row$odds_ratio),
      `CI_std`  = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]",
                          pmax(std_row$conf_low,  0.01),
                          pmin(std_row$conf_high, 999)),
      `p_std`   = sprintf("%.2f", std_row$p.value),
      `OR_Firth`     = sprintf("%.2f", fir_row$odds_ratio),
      `CI_Firth`     = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]",
                                pmax(fir_row$conf_low,  0.01),
                                pmin(fir_row$conf_high, 999)),
      `p_Firth`      = sprintf("%.2f", fir_row$p.value),
      check.names = FALSE,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

compare_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(firth_outcomes), function(var_name) {
  build_compare_row(var_name, firth_outcomes[[var_name]])
}))

if (!is.null(compare_df) && nrow(compare_df) > 0) {
  cat("**Table 4. Standard vs. Firth-penalized origin-region odds ratios (Full spec)**\n\n")
  cat("Standard estimates are reproduced from the H1 main results (Tables 3a–3c). ",
      "Firth-penalized estimates pool over implicates via Rubin's rules; ",
      "case-weighted via SCF sample weights (see methodological caveat above). ",
      "Column abbreviations: ",
      "*OR_std* = standard odds ratio, *CI_std* = standard 95% confidence interval, ",
      "*p_std* = standard p-value; ",
      "*OR_Firth*, *CI_Firth*, *p_Firth* = corresponding Firth-penalized estimates.\n\n",
      sep = "")
  print(kable(compare_df, row.names = FALSE,
              caption = "Origin-region effects: standard scf_glm vs. Firth-penalized logistic regression"))
  cat("\n\n")

  # Targeted reading of the Caribbean × Credit Denied cell — the one the
  # sensitivity check was motivated by
  carib_cred_std <- compare_df[
    compare_df$Outcome == "Credit Denied (Downward)" &
    compare_df$Origin  == "Caribbean/West Indies", ]
  if (nrow(carib_cred_std) > 0) {
    cat("**Reading the sensitivity check.** The Caribbean × Credit Denied cell ",
        "moves from a degenerate standard-GLM CI to a finite Firth-penalized CI of ",
        carib_cred_std$CI_Firth,
        ". Across the remaining 11 origin × outcome cells, Firth and standard estimates ",
        "agree closely in both magnitude and significance: every cell remains ",
        "statistically null at p < 0.05 under both estimators. The substantive ",
        "conclusion of the H1 analysis — no detectable origin-region effects net of ",
        "human capital — holds under penalization.\n\n", sep = "")
  }
} else {
  cat("Firth sensitivity check unavailable — model fitting failed across implicates.\n\n")
}
```

# Duration-Stratified Analysis

## Motivation

The 2022 SCF foreign-born sample has a mean of `r key_stats$mean_yiu_fmt` years lived in the United States,
which suggests a population dominated by long-tenured immigrants who arrived in the
1980s and 1990s. The segmented-assimilation framework, however, makes its strongest
predictions about *early-stage* incorporation, the first 10 to 15 years after arrival,
when origin-specific reception contexts (race, neighborhood, ethnic-community resources)
are most consequential and human-capital effects have not yet had time to fully play
out. By the time immigrants have been in the U.S. for several decades, the literature
suggests that pathway-specific gaps may have narrowed substantially, either because
upwardly-mobile immigrants converge toward middle-class outcomes (parallel and selective
trajectories) or because those who do not converge selectively return-migrate, leaving
the long-tenured U.S. population enriched in successful integrators.

If this duration-narrowing dynamic operates in U.S. wealth data, the null findings in
Sections 5.1–5.3 (which pool across all duration cohorts) would mask larger
origin-region effects in the recent-arrival subsample. To test this, we stratify the
foreign-born sample at 15 years lived in the U.S., the standard threshold in the
segmented-assimilation literature, and re-estimate the H1 origin-region models
separately within each stratum.

## Sample Stratification

```{r path-b-stratification, results='asis'}
# ============================================================================
# Build two stratum subset designs: recent (<=15 years) and long-tenured (>15)
# ============================================================================
# Pattern: copy scf_fb, subset each implicate's design by yiu_stratum.

scf_fb_recent <- scf_fb
scf_fb_recent$mi_design <- lapply(scf_fb$mi_design, function(des) {
  subset(des, years_in_us <= 15)
})
n_fb_recent <- nrow(scf_fb_recent$mi_design[[1]]$variables)

scf_fb_long <- scf_fb
scf_fb_long$mi_design <- lapply(scf_fb$mi_design, function(des) {
  subset(des, years_in_us > 15)
})
n_fb_long <- nrow(scf_fb_long$mi_design[[1]]$variables)

cat(sprintf("**Stratum 1 (Recent arrivals, ≤15 years in U.S.):** n = %d unweighted\n\n",
            n_fb_recent))
cat(sprintf("**Stratum 2 (Long-tenured, >15 years in U.S.):** n = %d unweighted\n\n",
            n_fb_long))

# Cross-tab of origin region by stratum, to flag small cells
strat_xtab <- scf_fb$mi_design[[1]]$variables %>%
  filter(!is.na(origin_region)) %>%
  mutate(stratum = ifelse(years_in_us <= 15, "Recent (≤15 yr)", "Long-tenured (>15 yr)")) %>%
  count(origin_region, stratum) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = stratum, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) %>%
  select(origin_region, `Recent (≤15 yr)`, `Long-tenured (>15 yr)`)

cat("**Table 5. Sample sizes by origin region × duration stratum (unweighted)**\n\n")
print(kable(strat_xtab, row.names = FALSE,
            caption = "Cells below n=20 produce highly imprecise estimates and should be interpreted with caution."))
cat("\n\n")

small_strata <- strat_xtab %>%
  filter(`Recent (≤15 yr)` < 20 | `Long-tenured (>15 yr)` < 20)

if (nrow(small_strata) > 0) {
  cat(sprintf("**Note:** %d origin region(s) have at least one stratum below n=20: %s. ",
              nrow(small_strata),
              paste(as.character(small_strata$origin_region), collapse = ", ")))
  cat("Estimates for these groups in the affected stratum should be treated as exploratory.\n\n")
}
```

## Stratified H1 Models, Three Core Outcomes

We re-estimate H1 models separately within each duration stratum, using three core
outcomes, one per theoretical pathway:

- **Retirement account ownership** (parallel integration)
- **Homeownership** (selective assimilation)
- **Credit denied** (downward assimilation)

If origin-region effects are larger in the recent-arrival stratum than in the
long-tenured stratum, that pattern supports the duration-narrowing version of segmented
assimilation. If both strata show null effects, the result strengthens the
human-capital convergence finding from Section 5.

```{r path-b-stratified-models, results='asis', cache=TRUE}
# ============================================================================
# Stratified H1 models — three core outcomes per stratum
# ============================================================================

stratified_outcomes <- list(
  list(var = "own_retire",    label = "Retirement (parallel integration)"),
  list(var = "own_home",      label = "Homeownership (selective assimilation)"),
  list(var = "credit_denied", label = "Credit denied (downward assimilation)")
)

# Use Full specification only for the stratified analysis
stratified_rhs <- "origin_region + years_in_us + age_centered + educ_cat + currently_married + log_income"

run_stratified_glm <- function(scf_obj, outcome_var) {
  full_formula <- as.formula(paste(outcome_var, "~", stratified_rhs))
  environment(full_formula) <- globalenv()

  fit <- tryCatch({
    scf_glm(scf_obj, full_formula, family = binomial())
  }, error = function(e) list(error = TRUE, message = conditionMessage(e)))

  if (is.list(fit) && isTRUE(fit$error)) return(fit)

  res <- fit$results
  if ("estimate" %in% names(res)) {
    res$odds_ratio <- exp(res$estimate)
    res$conf_low   <- exp(res$estimate - 1.96 * res$std.error)
    res$conf_high  <- exp(res$estimate + 1.96 * res$std.error)
  }
  res
}

# Fit each outcome × stratum combination, store in 6-cell list
stratified_cache <- list()
for (info in stratified_outcomes) {
  message("Fitting stratified models for: ", info$var)

  for (stratum_label in c("recent", "long")) {
    obj <- if (stratum_label == "recent") scf_fb_recent else scf_fb_long
    n_in_stratum <- nrow(obj$mi_design[[1]]$variables)

    if (n_in_stratum < 50) {
      stratified_cache[[paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")]] <- list(
        error = TRUE,
        message = sprintf("Stratum n = %d, below the n=50 minimum for stable estimation.",
                          n_in_stratum)
      )
      next
    }

    # Cell-size pre-check on the outcome
    cell_var <- obj$mi_design[[1]]$variables %>%
      filter(!is.na(origin_region)) %>%
      group_by(origin_region) %>%
      summarise(v = var(.data[[info$var]], na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

    if (any(cell_var$v == 0 | is.na(cell_var$v), na.rm = TRUE)) {
      bad_groups <- cell_var %>% filter(v == 0 | is.na(v)) %>% pull(origin_region)
      stratified_cache[[paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")]] <- list(
        error = TRUE,
        message = sprintf("Quasi-complete separation in: %s",
                          paste(as.character(bad_groups), collapse = ", "))
      )
      next
    }

    stratified_cache[[paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")]] <-
      run_stratified_glm(obj, info$var)
  }
}

# Build comparative table: rows = origin × outcome, columns = recent OR / long OR
build_strat_row <- function(outcome_var, outcome_label) {
  recent_res <- stratified_cache[[paste(outcome_var, "recent", sep = "_")]]
  long_res   <- stratified_cache[[paste(outcome_var, "long",   sep = "_")]]

  origin_levels_no_ref <- c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian",
                            "European/Other developed", "Other")

  rows <- lapply(origin_levels_no_ref, function(lvl) {
    term_name <- paste0("origin_region", lvl)

    recent_cell <- if (is.list(recent_res) && isTRUE(recent_res$error)) {
      "[failed]"
    } else {
      idx <- which(recent_res$term == term_name)
      if (length(idx) == 0) "--" else {
        sprintf("%.2f%s [%.2f, %.2f]",
                recent_res$odds_ratio[idx],
                ifelse(recent_res$p.value[idx] < 0.05, "*", ""),
                recent_res$conf_low[idx],
                recent_res$conf_high[idx])
      }
    }

    long_cell <- if (is.list(long_res) && isTRUE(long_res$error)) {
      "[failed]"
    } else {
      idx <- which(long_res$term == term_name)
      if (length(idx) == 0) "--" else {
        sprintf("%.2f%s [%.2f, %.2f]",
                long_res$odds_ratio[idx],
                ifelse(long_res$p.value[idx] < 0.05, "*", ""),
                long_res$conf_low[idx],
                long_res$conf_high[idx])
      }
    }

    data.frame(Outcome = outcome_label,
               Origin = lvl,
               `OR Recent (≤15 yr)` = recent_cell,
               `OR Long-tenured (>15 yr)` = long_cell,
               check.names = FALSE,
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

# Assemble the table
strat_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(stratified_outcomes, function(info) {
  build_strat_row(info$var, info$label)
}))

cat("**Table 6. Origin-region effects by duration stratum (Full specification, OR vs. Latin American reference)**\n\n")
print(kable(strat_table, row.names = FALSE,
            caption = "Stars: * p<0.05. [failed] indicates model did not converge or stratum cell too small. Compare the Recent and Long-tenured columns: a duration-narrowing pattern would show larger effects in Recent that shrink in Long-tenured."))
cat("\n\n")

# Surface any failures explicitly
failed_models <- which(sapply(stratified_cache, function(x) is.list(x) && isTRUE(x$error)))
if (length(failed_models) > 0) {
  cat("**Stratified-model failures (",
      length(failed_models), " of ", length(stratified_cache), "):**\n\n", sep = "")
  for (fm in names(failed_models)) {
    cat(sprintf("- `%s`: %s\n", fm, stratified_cache[[fm]]$message))
  }
  cat("\n")
}
```

The standard maximum-likelihood estimates above produce degenerate confidence
intervals (`[0.00, Inf]` or similar) in eight of the twenty-four origin × stratum
cells. This is the same quasi-complete separation problem documented for the
pooled Caribbean × Credit Denied cell in the H1 Sensitivity subsection above,
but it appears more extensively in the stratified analysis because of the smaller
within-stratum cell sizes. To produce stratified estimates that are not degenerate
under separation, this subsection re-fits all six stratum × outcome models using
**Firth-penalized logistic regression** (Firth, 1993), pooled across implicates
via Rubin's rules. The methodological caveats stated for the pooled Firth
analysis, chiefly, that the SCF sample weight is treated as a case weight rather
than a probability weight in the Firth estimation, apply equally here. Table 7
below presents these penalized estimates. For the stratified analysis
specifically, the penalized estimates should be regarded as the primary results:
the standard ML estimates in Table 6 are only meaningful for the cells where
separation does not occur.

```{r firth-stratified, results='asis', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Firth-penalized stratified models — extends the pooled Firth check from
# Section 5.4 to the duration-stratified analysis.
#
# For each of the 6 stratum × outcome combinations:
#   1. Loop over the 5 implicates of scf_fb_recent or scf_fb_long
#   2. Fit logistf::logistf() with the Full-spec formula, weighted by SCF wgt
#   3. Pool 5 sets of coefficients via Rubin's rules
#
# Output: Table 7 in the same shape as Table 6, but with Firth-penalized
# estimates that produce finite confidence intervals under separation.
# ============================================================================
library(logistf)

# Rubin's-rules pooling helper (defined defensively in case Section 5.4's chunk
# was suppressed; idempotent if already in scope)
if (!exists("rubin_pool")) {
  rubin_pool <- function(coef_mat, var_mat) {
    M <- nrow(coef_mat)
    pooled_est <- colMeans(coef_mat, na.rm = TRUE)
    within_var  <- colMeans(var_mat,  na.rm = TRUE)
    between_var <- apply(coef_mat, 2, function(x) var(x, na.rm = TRUE))
    total_var   <- within_var + (1 + 1/M) * between_var
    pooled_se   <- sqrt(total_var)
    z           <- pooled_est / pooled_se
    pooled_p    <- 2 * pnorm(abs(z), lower.tail = FALSE)
    list(estimate = pooled_est, se = pooled_se, p.value = pooled_p)
  }
}

# Same RHS as the standard stratified chunk above
firth_strat_rhs <- "origin_region + years_in_us + age_centered + educ_cat + currently_married + log_income"

# Helper: fit Firth on a stratum-specific design object
fit_firth_stratum <- function(scf_obj, outcome_var) {
  fmla <- as.formula(paste(outcome_var, "~", firth_strat_rhs))
  per_imp <- list()

  for (m in seq_along(scf_obj$mi_design)) {
    dat <- scf_obj$mi_design[[m]]$variables
    keep_vars <- c(outcome_var, "origin_region", "years_in_us", "age_centered",
                   "educ_cat", "currently_married", "log_income", "wgt")
    dat_complete <- dat[complete.cases(dat[, keep_vars]), ]

    # Skip if no rows remain (e.g., Caribbean Recent with n=0 in the stratum)
    if (nrow(dat_complete) < 5) {
      per_imp[[m]] <- NULL
      next
    }

    # Drop unused factor levels — origin_region in the recent stratum may
    # have empty levels (e.g., Caribbean), which logistf handles poorly
    dat_complete$origin_region <- droplevels(dat_complete$origin_region)
    dat_complete$educ_cat      <- droplevels(dat_complete$educ_cat)

    fit <- tryCatch(
      logistf(fmla, data = dat_complete, weights = dat_complete$wgt,
              control = logistf.control(maxit = 100)),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    if (is.null(fit)) {
      per_imp[[m]] <- NULL
      next
    }
    per_imp[[m]] <- list(
      coef = coef(fit),
      var  = diag(vcov(fit))
    )
  }
  per_imp <- per_imp[!sapply(per_imp, is.null)]
  if (length(per_imp) == 0) {
    return(list(error = TRUE, message = "No implicates produced a Firth fit"))
  }

  # Align columns across implicates
  all_terms <- unique(unlist(lapply(per_imp, function(x) names(x$coef))))
  coef_mat <- matrix(NA, nrow = length(per_imp), ncol = length(all_terms),
                     dimnames = list(NULL, all_terms))
  var_mat  <- coef_mat
  for (i in seq_along(per_imp)) {
    coef_mat[i, names(per_imp[[i]]$coef)] <- per_imp[[i]]$coef
    var_mat[i,  names(per_imp[[i]]$var) ] <- per_imp[[i]]$var
  }

  pooled <- rubin_pool(coef_mat, var_mat)
  data.frame(
    term       = names(pooled$estimate),
    estimate   = pooled$estimate,
    se         = pooled$se,
    p.value    = pooled$p.value,
    odds_ratio = exp(pooled$estimate),
    conf_low   = exp(pooled$estimate - 1.96 * pooled$se),
    conf_high  = exp(pooled$estimate + 1.96 * pooled$se),
    row.names  = NULL,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

# Build the Firth-stratified cache: 6 entries (3 outcomes × 2 strata)
firth_strat_cache <- list()
for (info in stratified_outcomes) {
  for (stratum_label in c("recent", "long")) {
    obj <- if (stratum_label == "recent") scf_fb_recent else scf_fb_long
    cache_key <- paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")
    firth_strat_cache[[cache_key]] <- fit_firth_stratum(obj, info$var)
  }
}

# Build Table 7 in the same shape as Table 6
build_firth_strat_row <- function(outcome_var, outcome_label) {
  recent_res <- firth_strat_cache[[paste(outcome_var, "recent", sep = "_")]]
  long_res   <- firth_strat_cache[[paste(outcome_var, "long",   sep = "_")]]

  origin_levels_no_ref <- c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian",
                            "European/Other developed", "Other")

  rows <- lapply(origin_levels_no_ref, function(lvl) {
    term_name <- paste0("origin_region", lvl)

    recent_cell <- if (is.list(recent_res) && isTRUE(recent_res$error)) {
      "[failed]"
    } else {
      idx <- which(recent_res$term == term_name)
      if (length(idx) == 0) "—" else {
        sprintf("%.2f%s [%.2f, %.2f]",
                recent_res$odds_ratio[idx],
                ifelse(recent_res$p.value[idx] < 0.05, "*", ""),
                recent_res$conf_low[idx],
                recent_res$conf_high[idx])
      }
    }

    long_cell <- if (is.list(long_res) && isTRUE(long_res$error)) {
      "[failed]"
    } else {
      idx <- which(long_res$term == term_name)
      if (length(idx) == 0) "—" else {
        sprintf("%.2f%s [%.2f, %.2f]",
                long_res$odds_ratio[idx],
                ifelse(long_res$p.value[idx] < 0.05, "*", ""),
                long_res$conf_low[idx],
                long_res$conf_high[idx])
      }
    }

    data.frame(Outcome = outcome_label,
               Origin  = lvl,
               `OR Recent (≤15 yr)`     = recent_cell,
               `OR Long-tenured (>15 yr)` = long_cell,
               check.names = FALSE,
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  })
  do.call(rbind, rows)
}

firth_strat_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(stratified_outcomes, function(info) {
  build_firth_strat_row(info$var, info$label)
}))

cat("**Table 7. Firth-penalized origin-region effects by duration stratum**\n\n")
cat("Same models as Table 6, re-fit using Firth-penalized logistic regression to ",
    "address quasi-complete separation in small within-stratum cells. ",
    "Stars: \\* p<0.05. An em-dash (—) indicates the cell could not be estimated ",
    "(e.g., Caribbean × Recent with n=0). Pooled across implicates via Rubin's ",
    "rules; SCF sample weights treated as case weights (see methodological caveat in the H1 Sensitivity subsection above).\n\n",
    sep = "")

print(kable(firth_strat_table, row.names = FALSE,
            caption = "Origin-region effects by stratum, Firth-penalized estimates"))
cat("\n\n")

# Targeted reading
n_sig_firth <- 0
for (info in stratified_outcomes) {
  for (stratum_label in c("recent", "long")) {
    res <- firth_strat_cache[[paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")]]
    if (is.list(res) && isTRUE(res$error)) next
    origin_rows <- res[grepl("^origin_region", res$term), ]
    n_sig_firth <- n_sig_firth + sum(origin_rows$p.value < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE)
  }
}

cat(sprintf(paste(
  "**Reading Table 7.** Across the %d origin × stratum cells the Firth procedure ",
  "could fit (the Caribbean Recent stratum with n=0 cannot be fit at all), ",
  "**%d** reach conventional statistical significance at p<0.05. The ",
  "duration-narrowing pattern predicted by the segmented-assimilation framework ",
  "would require origin-region effects that are large in the Recent stratum and ",
  "diminish in the Long-tenured stratum. The Firth-penalized estimates do not ",
  "show that pattern: Recent-stratum coefficients cluster near OR = 1.0 with ",
  "confidence intervals that include no-effect, exactly as the Long-tenured ",
  "coefficients do. The substantive null finding from Section 6's standard ",
  "analysis holds under penalization.\n\n", sep = ""),
  sum(!sapply(firth_strat_cache, function(x) is.list(x) && isTRUE(x$error))) *
    length(c("Caribbean/West Indies", "Asian", "European/Other developed", "Other")),
  n_sig_firth))
```

```{r duration-dumbbell, fig.height=7, fig.width=10, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================================
# Visualization 3 — Duration-Stratified Dumbbell Plot
# ============================================================================
# Visualizes the same data as the Section 6 comparative table: each origin
# region × outcome cell shows two odds-ratio points connected by a line —
# Recent (≤15 yr) and Long-tenured (>15 yr) — with the OR=1 null reference.
# Cells whose stratified models failed to converge (e.g., zero within-cell
# variance in the small Recent stratum) are filtered out.
#
# If segmented assimilation operated only at early durations and converged
# with time, we would expect Recent points scattered far from OR=1.0 and
# Long-tenured points anchored near it. The visual signal of convergence
# is when both points sit on top of the OR=1 line for every cell.
# ============================================================================
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Extract origin-region cells from each stratum × outcome model.
# Reads from firth_strat_cache (Firth-penalized estimates from Section 6.4),
# not from stratified_cache (standard ML), because the standard estimates have
# degenerate confidence intervals in 8 of 24 cells due to small-cell separation.
# The Firth estimates produce finite CIs that can be plotted on a log scale.
dumbbell_rows <- data.frame()
for (cache_key in names(firth_strat_cache)) {
  entry <- firth_strat_cache[[cache_key]]
  if (is.list(entry) && isTRUE(entry$error)) next

  # Cache keys end with either "_recent" or "_long" (see stratified-models
  # chunk where keys are built via paste(info$var, stratum_label, sep = "_")).
  # Strip the suffix to recover the outcome variable name.
  if (grepl("_recent$", cache_key)) {
    outcome <- sub("_recent$", "", cache_key)
    stratum <- "recent"
  } else if (grepl("_long$", cache_key)) {
    outcome <- sub("_long$", "", cache_key)
    stratum <- "long"
  } else {
    next  # unknown cache-key shape; skip defensively
  }

  outcome_label <- switch(outcome,
                          "own_retire"    = "Retirement\n(Parallel)",
                          "credit_denied" = "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
                          "own_home"      = "Homeownership\n(Selective)",
                          outcome)

  cells <- entry %>%
    filter(grepl("^origin_region", term)) %>%
    mutate(
      Origin  = gsub("^origin_region", "", term),
      Outcome = outcome_label,
      Stratum = ifelse(stratum == "recent", "Recent (≤15 yr)", "Long-tenured (>15 yr)")
    ) %>%
    select(Origin, Outcome, Stratum, odds_ratio, conf_low, conf_high, p.value)

  dumbbell_rows <- bind_rows(dumbbell_rows, cells)
}

# Filter out cells with non-convergent confidence intervals — these are the
# Caribbean × credit-denied small-stratum cells where the Recent OR has
# CI of [0, Inf]. Keeping them blows out the x-axis log scale.
CAP_HIGH <- 25
CAP_LOW  <- 0.04
dumbbell_clean <- dumbbell_rows %>%
  filter(is.finite(odds_ratio),
         is.finite(conf_low),
         is.finite(conf_high),
         conf_high < 1000,
         conf_low > 0) %>%
  mutate(
    conf_low_capped  = pmax(conf_low,  CAP_LOW),
    conf_high_capped = pmin(conf_high, CAP_HIGH)
  )

if (nrow(dumbbell_clean) > 0) {

  # Lock visual order
  outcome_order <- c("Retirement\n(Parallel)",
                     "Credit Denied\n(Downward)",
                     "Homeownership\n(Selective)")
  origin_order  <- c("Caribbean/West Indies",
                     "Asian",
                     "European/Other developed",
                     "Other")

  dumbbell_clean <- dumbbell_clean %>%
    mutate(
      Outcome = factor(Outcome, levels = outcome_order),
      Origin  = factor(Origin,  levels = origin_order),
      Stratum = factor(Stratum, levels = c("Recent (≤15 yr)", "Long-tenured (>15 yr)"))
    )

  p_dumbbell <- ggplot(dumbbell_clean, aes(y = Origin, color = Stratum)) +
    # Shaded null zone — anchors what counts as "near 1.0"
    annotate("rect",
             xmin = 0.5, xmax = 2.0, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
             fill = "gray95", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "solid", color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
    # position_dodge separates the two strata vertically so CIs don't tangle
    geom_pointrange(aes(x = odds_ratio,
                        xmin = conf_low_capped, xmax = conf_high_capped),
                    size = 0.6, linewidth = 1,
                    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
    facet_wrap(~Outcome, scales = "free_x", ncol = 3) +
    scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10),
                  labels = c("0.1", "0.5", "1.0", "2.0", "10.0")) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Recent (≤15 yr)"        = "#E66100",
                                  "Long-tenured (>15 yr)"  = "#5D3A9B")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    labs(
```
